subject: Mathematics
q: An unbiased coin is tossed. If the outcome is a head then a pair of unbiased dice is rolled and the sum of the numbers obtained on them is noted. If the toss of the coin results in tail then a card from a well-shuffled pack of nine cards numbered <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>9</mn></math> is randomly picked and the number on the card is noted. The probability that the noted number is either <math><mn>7</mn></math> or <math><mn>8</mn></math> is
question: An unbiased coin is tossed. If the outcome is a head then a pair of unbiased dice is rolled and the sum of the numbers obtained on them is noted. If the toss of the coin results in tail then a card from a well-shuffled pack of nine cards numbered <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1,</mn><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>9</mn></math> is randomly picked and the number on the card is noted. The probability that the noted number is either <math><mn>7</mn></math> or <math><mn>8</mn></math> is
solution:
Let, A be the event that the noted number is either 7 or 8.

And, E1 be the event that the toss of coin results in head and E2 be the event that the toss of coin results in tail.

Then, we have PE1=PE2=12

And, PA|E1=P(getting the sum of the numbers on the pair of dice as 7 or 8)

The total number of cases, when a pair of dice is thrown are =6×6=36 and the cases of getting sum 7 or 8 are 1, 6, 2, 5, 3, 4, 4, 3, 5, 2, 6, 1, 2, 6, 3, 5, 4, 4, 5, 3, 6, 2=11 cases.

⇒PA|E1=1136

Similarly, PA|E2=(getting the number 7 or 8 from the numbers 1, 2, 3,.., 9)

⇒PA|E2=29

Now, using the total probability theorem, we get

PA=PE1PA|E1+PE1PA|E2

⇒PA=12×1136+12×29

⇒PA=1172+19=1972.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let there be three independent events E1,E2 and E3. The probability that only E1 occurs is α only E2 occurs is β and only E3 occurs is γ. Let p'' denote the probability of none of events occurs that satisfies the equations (α-2β)p=αβ and (β-3γ)p=2βγ. All the
given probabilities are assumed to lie in the interval 0, 1.

Then,  Probability of occurrence of E1 Probability of occurrence of E3 is equal to ________.
question:
Let there be three independent events E1,E2 and E3. The probability that only E1 occurs is α only E2 occurs is β and only E3 occurs is γ. Let p'' denote the probability of none of events occurs that satisfies the equations (α-2β)p=αβ and (β-3γ)p=2βγ. All the
given probabilities are assumed to lie in the interval 0, 1.

Then,  Probability of occurrence of E1 Probability of occurrence of E3 is equal to ________.
solution:
Let PE1=P1;PE2=P2;PE3=P3

PE1∩E¯2∩E¯3=α=P11-P21-P3 …1

PE¯1∩E2∩E¯3=β=1-P1P21-P3 …2

PE¯1∩E¯2∩E3=γ=1-P11-P2P3 …3

PE¯1∩E¯2∩E¯3=p=1-P11-P21-P3 …4

Given that, (α-2β)p=αβ

⇒P11-P21-P3-21-P1P21-P3p=P1P2

1-P11-P21-P32

⇒P11-P2-21-P1P2=P1P2

⇒P1-P1P2-2P2+2P1P2=P1P2

⇒P1=2P2 …1

and similarly, (β-3γ)P=2βγ

P2=3P3 …2

So, P1=6P3⇒P1P3=6

subject: Mathematics
q: Events $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ are mutually exclusive events such that $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{3 \mathrm{x}+1}{3}, \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{C})=\frac{1-2 \mathrm{x}}{4}$. The set of possible values of $\mathrm{x}$ are in the interval.
question: Events $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ are mutually exclusive events such that $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{3 \mathrm{x}+1}{3}, \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{C})=\frac{1-2 \mathrm{x}}{4}$. The set of possible values of $\mathrm{x}$ are in the interval.
solution:
$\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})=\frac{3 \mathrm{x}+1}{3}, \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=\frac{1-\mathrm{x}}{4}, \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{C})=\frac{1-2 \mathrm{x}}{2}$
These are mutually exclusive
$0 \leq \frac{3 x+1}{3} \leq 1, \quad 0 \leq \frac{1-x}{4} \leq 1$ and $0 \leq \frac{1-2 x}{2} \leq 1$
$-1 \leq 3 x \leq 2,-3 \leq x \leq 1$ and $-1 \leq 2 x \leq 1$
$-\frac{1}{3} \leq x \leq \frac{2}{3},-3 \leq x \leq 1$, and $-\frac{1}{2} \leq x \leq \frac{1}{2}$
Also $0 \leq \frac{1+3 x}{3}+\frac{1-x}{4}+\frac{1-2 x}{2} \leq 1$
$0 \leq 13-3 x \leq 12 \Rightarrow 1 \leq 3 x \leq 13 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \leq x \leq \frac{13}{3}$
$\max \left\{-\frac{1}{3},-3,-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}\right\} \leq \mathrm{x} \leq \min \left\{\frac{2}{3}, 1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{13}{3}\right\}$
$\frac{1}{3} \leq x \leq \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x \in\left[\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{2}\right]$

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider 5 independent Bernoulli's trials each with probability of success $\mathrm{p}$. If the probability of at least one failure is greater than or equal to $\frac{31}{32}$, then $\mathrm{p}$ lies in the interval
question: Consider 5 independent Bernoulli's trials each with probability of success $\mathrm{p}$. If the probability of at least one failure is greater than or equal to $\frac{31}{32}$, then $\mathrm{p}$ lies in the interval
solution:
$\mathrm{n}=5$
Success $=\mathrm{p}$
Failure $=\mathrm{q}$
$\mathrm{P}$ (at least one failure) $\geq \frac{31}{32}$
$1-\mathrm{P}$ (no failure) $\geq \frac{31}{32}$
$1-\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x}=5) \geq \frac{31}{32}$
$1-{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{p}^5 \geq \frac{31}{32}$
$-\mathrm{p}^5 \geq-\frac{1}{32}$
$\mathrm{p}^5 \leq \frac{1}{32}$
$\mathrm{p} \leq \frac{1}{2}$
$\mathrm{p} \in\left[0, \frac{1}{2}\right]$

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the population are smokers. A smoker has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> times more chances to develop lung cancer then a non-smoker. A person is diagnosed with lung cancer and the probability that this person is a smoker is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>k</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>.Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ .
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the population are smokers. A smoker has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> times more chances to develop lung cancer then a non-smoker. A person is diagnosed with lung cancer and the probability that this person is a smoker is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>k</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>.Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Given,

In a city, 25% of the population is smoker

So, Psmoker=0.25

Pnon-smoking=0.75

And smoker has 27 times more chance of being diagnosed with lung cancer,

So, if PLung cancernon-smoker=p then PLung cancersmoker=27p,

Now,  a person is selected at random and found to be diagnosed with lung cancer,

So, total probability of having a lung cancer will be 

PLung cancer=0.25×27p+0.75×p

So the probability of him being smoker (using bayes' theorem) will be PsmokerLung cancer=0.25×27p0.25×27p+0.75×p=2730=910

Now comparing with k10=910,

Then the value of k will be 9.

subject: Mathematics
q: A pair of dice is thrown 5 times. For each throw, a total of 5 is considered a success. If the probability of at least 4 successes is k311, then k is equal to
question: A pair of dice is thrown 5 times. For each throw, a total of 5 is considered a success. If the probability of at least 4 successes is k311, then k is equal to
solution:
Given that,

A pair of dice is thrown 5 times.

Required Sample space is 1,1,1,2,1,3...........6,6

Total number of observations =36

Now, a sum of 5 is observed in 1,4,4,1,2,3,3,2

No of favourable outcomes =4

P(success) p=436=19

P(failure) q=1-19=89

We know that for a binomial distribution, Bn,p

PX=k=Cknpkqn-k where k≥0 and p+q=1

∴Required probability

⇒PX≥4=PX=4+PX=5

=C4519489+C55195

=5·895+195=4195

=41×3310×3=123311

But given that PX≥4=k311

⇒k311=123311

⇒k=123

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: A student appeared in an examination consisting of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> true-false type questions. The student guesses the answers with equal probability. The smallest value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo></math> so that the probability of guessing at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> correct answers is less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> is :
question: A student appeared in an examination consisting of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> true-false type questions. The student guesses the answers with equal probability. The smallest value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo></math> so that the probability of guessing at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> correct answers is less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> is :
solution:
PE<12

⇒∑r=n8Cr8128-r12r<12

⇒∑r=n8Cr8128<12

⇒Cn8+Cn+18+…+C88<128

⇒256-C08+C18+…+Cn-18<128

⇒C08+C18+…+Cn-18>128

⇒1+8+28+56+70+C58+…+Cn-18>128

⇒163+C58+…+Cn-18>128

⇒n-1≥4

⇒n≥5

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math>, be two events such that the probability that exactly one of them occurs is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, and the probability that <math><mi>A</mi></math> or <math><mi>B</mi></math>, occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then the probability of both of them occur together is.
question: Let <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math>, be two events such that the probability that exactly one of them occurs is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, and the probability that <math><mi>A</mi></math> or <math><mi>B</mi></math>, occurs is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, then the probability of both of them occur together is.
solution:
P (exactly one) =25
=PA+PB-2PA∩B=25  ...1
PA∪B=12
=PA+PB-PA∩B=12  ...2
∴PA∩B=12-25=5-410=110.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the numbers appeared on the two throws of a fair six faced die are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>, then the probability that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, is
question: If the numbers appeared on the two throws of a fair six faced die are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>, then the probability that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, is
solution:
Total sample space of two dice will be equal to 6×6=36

Now for x2+αx+β>0 its discriminant D must be less than zero,

So D=b2-4ac<0 or α2-4β<0⇒α2<4β 

Now, if α=1,β can take values 1,2,3,4,5,6

If α=2,β can take values 2,3,4,5,6

If α=3,β can take values 3,4,5,6

If α=4,β can take values 5,6

If α=5 and 6, no value of β possible

So number of favourable ways =17

Required probability=favourable waystotal sample space=1736

subject: Mathematics
q:
If a random variable $x$ has the probability distribution
$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline x & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 \\ \hline P(x) & 0 & 2k & k & 3k & 2k^{2} & 2k & k^{2}+k & 7k^{2} \\ \hline \end{array}$
then $P(3 < x \leq 6)$ is equal to
question:
If a random variable $x$ has the probability distribution
$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline x & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 \\ \hline P(x) & 0 & 2k & k & 3k & 2k^{2} & 2k & k^{2}+k & 7k^{2} \\ \hline \end{array}$
then $P(3 < x \leq 6)$ is equal to
solution:
Sum of all probabilities = 1
$\Rightarrow 0 + 2k + k + 3k + 2k^2 + 2k + (k^2+k) + 7k^2 = 1$
$\Rightarrow 10k^2 + 9k - 1 = 0$.
$\Rightarrow(10k-1)(k+1) = 0$
$\Rightarrow k = \frac{1}{10}$ (rejecting $k = -1$).
$P(3 < x \le 6) = P(4) + P(5) + P(6)$
$= 2k^2 + 2k + k^2 + k$
$= 3k^2 + 3k = 3(0.01) + 3(0.1) = 0.33$.

subject: Mathematics
q: A bag contains 19 unbiased coins and one coin with head on both sides. One coin drawn at random is tossed and head turns up. If the probability that the drawn coin was unbiased, is $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}, \operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$, then $\mathrm{n}^2-\mathrm{m}^2$ is equal to :
question: A bag contains 19 unbiased coins and one coin with head on both sides. One coin drawn at random is tossed and head turns up. If the probability that the drawn coin was unbiased, is $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}, \operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$, then $\mathrm{n}^2-\mathrm{m}^2$ is equal to :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Required probability }=\frac{\frac{19}{20} \times \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{19}{20} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{20} \times 1}=\frac{19}{21} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{19}{21} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^2-\mathrm{m}^2=441-361=80\end{aligned}$

question: The probability of a man hitting a target is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The least number of shots required, so that the probability of his hitting the target at least once is greater than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> is....
solution:
p=110, q=910

P (not hitting in n trials) =910n

∴ P (at least one hit)=1−910n>14

⇒ 910n<34

0.9n<0.75

If n=3 ⇒ 0.729<0.75 which is true.

q: A fair coin is tossed a fixed number of times. If the probability of getting <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> heads is equal to probability of getting <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> heads, then the probability of getting <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> heads is<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
question: A fair coin is tossed a fixed number of times. P(2 heads)=15213

q: The probability, that in a randomly selected <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit number at least two digits are odd, is
question: The probability, that in a randomly selected <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit number at least two digits are odd, is
solution:
Total No of 3-digit Number =900

Case -1 When there are 2 odd digit and 1 even digit

(i) Even odd odd =4×5×5=100

{As 0 cannot come at 1st place}

(ii) Odd even odd =5×5×5=125

(iii) Odd odd even =5×5×5=125

Case-2

All 3 odd digit =5×5×5=125

So total number of selecting 3-digit number having atleast 2 odd digit will be addition of both cases which is=100+125+125+125=475

So probability =475900=1936

question: The mean and variance of a binomial distribution are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> respectively. q: An urn contains nine balls of which three are red, four are blue and two are green. Three balls are drawn at random without replacement from the urn. The probability that the three balls have different colour is
question: An urn contains nine balls of which three are red, four are blue and two are green. Three balls are drawn at random without replacement from the urn. The probability that the three balls have different colour is
solution:
$\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{S})={ }^9 \mathrm{C}_3$
$\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{E})={ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^4 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1$
Probability $=\frac{3 \times 4 \times 2}{{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_3}=\frac{24 \times 3 !}{9 !} \times 6 !=\frac{24 \times 6}{9 \times 8 \times 7}=\frac{2}{7}$

solution:
$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline x & 0 & 1 & -1 \\
\hline P(x) & \frac{10}{16} & \frac{3}{16} & \frac{3}{16} \\
\hline
\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} & \operatorname{Var}(x)=E\left(x^2\right)-[E(x)]^2 \\ & =\sum_{i=1}^3 x_i^2 P\left(x_i\right)-(\mu)^2 \\ & =1 \times \frac{3}{16}+1 \times \frac{3}{16} \quad[\mu=0] \\ & =\frac{6}{16}=\frac{3}{8}\end{aligned}$

question: Five horses are in a race. Mr. A selects two of the horses at random and bets on them. The probability that Mr. A selected the winning horse is
solution:
$\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{S})={ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 ; \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{E})={ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1$
$\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{E})=\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{E})}{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{S})}=\frac{{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_1}{{ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{2}{5}$

q: A bag contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> balls. Two balls are drawn from it at random and both are found to be black. The probability that the bag contains at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> black balls is
question: A bag contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> balls. Two balls are drawn from it at random and both are found to be black. The probability that the bag contains at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> black balls is
solution:
Let the bag contains black ballsB and non-black balls B¯, then we can have the following possibilities:

6B,0B¯, 5B,1B¯, 4B,2B¯, 3B,3B¯,2B,4B¯

All the events are equally likely, i.e., 15.

Required probability

=15×C26C26+15×C25C2615×C26C26+15×C25C26+15×C24C26+15×C23C26+15×C22C26

=2535=57

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> have a binomial distribution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> such that the sum and the product of the mean and variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>128</mn></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>X</mi><mo>></mo><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></msup></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> have a binomial distribution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> such that the sum and the product of the mean and variance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>128</mn></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>X</mi><mo>></mo><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi></msup></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Let M=Mean and V=Variance M>V

So, M+V=24, MV=128

⇒M=16 and V=8

Now, mean np=16

and variance npq=8⇒q=12

∴  p=12,n=32

Now PX>n-3=12nCn-2n+Cn-1n+Cnn

∴ k2n=12nC3032+C3132+C3232

⇒k=32×312+32+1

=496+33=529

subject: Mathematics
q: A bag contains six balls of different colours. Two balls are drawn in succession with replacement. The probability that both the balls are of the same colour is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>. Next four balls are drawn in succession with replacement and the probability that exactly three balls are of the same colours is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>:</mo><mi>n</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> are co-prime, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to
question: A bag contains six balls of different colours. Two balls are drawn in succession with replacement. The probability that both the balls are of the same colour is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>. Next four balls are drawn in succession with replacement and the probability that exactly three balls are of the same colours is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>:</mo><mi>n</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> are co-prime, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

A bag contains six balls of different colours, two balls are drawn in succession with replacement,

The probability that both the balls are of the same colour will be p=636

Now Next four balls are drawn in succession with replacement and the probability that exactly three balls are of the same colours is q,

So, probability will be q=C16×C15×C1464=1201296=554

Now the ratio of p & q will be pq=95

Now, on comparing with pq=mn⇒m+n=14

subject: Mathematics
q: Bag<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> white, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> red balls and bag <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> white, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> red balls. One bag is selected at random and a ball is drawn from it. The probability of drawing the ball from the bag <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, if the ball drawn in white, is :
question: Bag<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> white, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math> red balls and bag <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> white, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> red balls. One bag is selected at random and a ball is drawn from it. The probability of drawing the ball from the bag <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, if the ball drawn in white, is :
solution:
Given: Given balls in bags are A→3W, 7R, B→3W, 2R

Let, E1:A is selected

, E2:B is selected

and E: white ball is drawn

Using Baye's theorem,
⇒PE1E=P(E1)·PEE1PE1·PEE1+PE2·PEE2

⇒PE1E=12×31012×310+12×35

⇒PE1E=320320+620

⇒PE1E=33+6

⇒PE1E=13

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mi> </mi></math> be two non-null events such that <math><mi>A</mi><mo>⊂</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, which of the following statements is always correct?
question: Let <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi><mi> </mi></math> be two non-null events such that <math><mi>A</mi><mo>⊂</mo><mi>B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, which of the following statements is always correct?
solution:
Lets draw the venn diagram according to question, 

PAB=P(A∩B)P(B)=P(A)P(B)      [A⊂B⇒A∩B=A]

Now, since P(B)≤1

⇒PAB=PAPB≥PA

⇒PA|B≥P(A)

subject: Mathematics
q: In a game, a man wins Rs. <math><mn>100</mn></math> if he gets <math><mn>5</mn></math> or <math><mn>6</mn></math> on a throw of a fair die and loses Rs. <math><mn>50</mn></math> for getting any other number on the die. If he decides to throw the die either till he gets a five or a six or to a maximum of three throws, then his expected gain/loss (in rupees) is :
question: In a game, a man wins Rs. <math><mn>100</mn></math> if he gets <math><mn>5</mn></math> or <math><mn>6</mn></math> on a throw of a fair die and loses Rs. <math><mn>50</mn></math> for getting any other number on the die. If he decides to throw the die either till he gets a five or a six or to a maximum of three throws, then his expected gain/loss (in rupees) is :
solution:
Probability of success i.e. getting a 5 or 6=Ps=26=13.

So, probability of failure i.e. getting any number other than 5 or 6=Pf=1-Ps=1-13=23.

Let X be the amount that a man wins in a maximum of three throws.

So, X can take values :

Case I : First two throws are 5 or 6, game stops

⇒X=100⇒PX=100=13

Case II : First throw is other than 5 or 6 and second and third throws are 5 or 6, game stops

⇒X=50⇒PX=50=23×13=29

Case III : First two throws are other than 5 or 6 and last throw is 5 or 6

⇒X=0⇒PX=0=23×23×13=427

Case IV : All three throws are other than 5 or 6

⇒X=-150⇒PX=-150=23×23×23=827

Therefore, expected gain or loss

=EX=100×13+50×29+0×427-150×827=0

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub><mo>∈</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>j</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> be a sample space and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>M</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>is invertible</mtext></mrow></mfenced></math> be an even. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>A</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub><mo>∈</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>j</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> be a sample space and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>M</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>is invertible</mtext></mrow></mfenced></math> be an even. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>A</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

S=M=aij, aij∈0,1,2, 1≤i,j≤2 

So, let M=abcd where a,b,c,d∈0,1,2

So, total sample space will be 34=81

Now we will find PA¯ or possibilities for |M|=0

So, for |M|=0 cases will be,

1.1111 or 0000 or 2222→ Total matrix =3
2. Two 1's and Two 0's → Total matrix =4
3. Two 2's and Two 0's → Total matrix =4
4. Two 1's and Two 2's → Total matrix=4
5. One 1 and three 0 's → Total matrix =4
6. One 2 and Three 0's → Total matrix =4
7. One 1 and one 2 and two 0's → Total matrix =8

So, total cases for which M=0 is 31,

So probability will be PA¯=3181
Hence, P(A)=1-3181=5081

subject: Mathematics
q: If the probability that a randomly chosen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>-digit number formed by using digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> only is a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>96</mn><mi>p</mi></math> is equal to _____.
question: If the probability that a randomly chosen <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>-digit number formed by using digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> only is a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>96</mn><mi>p</mi></math> is equal to _____.
solution:
We have to choose 6 digit number by using 1 & 8 and it must be divisible by 21,

So, equal number of digits 1 and 8 should be used to form a number divisible by 3 and 7. (Or all the digits are either 1 or 8)

In all cases whatever number is formed using six 1' or six 8's or three 1's + three 8's, that number can be splited into sum of multiples of 1001,1008,8001,8008 (all of them are divisible by 7). So, all the numbers thus formed will be divisible by 21,

Example: 811188=8001×102+1008×10+1008

Number of such numbers =6!3!3!+2=22

Required Probability, p=2226=1132

∴ 96p=1122×96=33

subject: Mathematics
q: A bag contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> balls, whose colours are either white or black. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> balls are drawn at random without replacement and it was found that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> balls are white and other <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> balls are black. The probability that the bag contains equal number of white and black balls is:
question: A bag contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> balls, whose colours are either white or black. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> balls are drawn at random without replacement and it was found that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> balls are white and other <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> balls are black. The probability that the bag contains equal number of white and black balls is:
solution:
Total Possible cases of balls in the bag could be:

2W,6B,3W,5B,4W,4B,5W,3B,6W,2B

The favourable case is to choose 2W and 2B from the bag having 4W,4B

So, the required probability is given by

PE=C24×C24C28C22×C26C28+C23×C25C28+C24×C24C28+C25×C23C28+C26×C22C28

⇒PE=6×615+3×10+36+30+15

⇒PE=36126

⇒PE=27
